perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such documentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be returned within 30 days of the end of the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such documentation' to 'returned within 30 days' introduces an in-text contradiction. The removal of the requirement of sending copies of the documentation contradicts section 3, which dictates the protection of the secrecy of and avoid disclosure of confidential information. The altered clause removes the certainty that copies are also being returned, potentially enabling the Recipient to keep the copies without violating this section. The 'promptly' clause is being replaced with '30 days' creating also uncertainty. This introduces ambiguity about the Recipient's obligation regarding documentation after the evaluation, creating a conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient agr ees not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not disclose any Confidential Informati on to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to carry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non-Di sclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed. Recipient agrees that it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the return of materials 'accompanied by copies', ensuring the Discloser retained a record. The changed text omits this requirement, only mandating return 'within 30 days'. This omission contradicts the Contradicted Text's emphasis on protecting confidential information and preventing its disclosure. Without the requirement to return copies, the Recipient could potentially retain the confidential information, violating the spirit and intent of the Contradicted Text, which demands measures to 'protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information'. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction', where essential language is removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated elsewhere. The omission of returning copies directly weakens the confidentiality protection measures outlined in the Contradicted Text.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of three (3) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of one (1) year following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the term of confidentiality from 'three (3) years' to 'one (1) year' introduces an in-text contradiction with Section 10, which states that Recipient's obligations are necessary and reasonable to protect the Discloser's business. Reducing the term may not provide adequate protection, thereby contradicting the assertion that the obligations are necessary and reasonable, leading to uncertainty in the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that its obliga tions hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. A ccordingly, Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the confidentiality term from three years to one year. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the recipient's obligations are 'necessary and reasonable' to protect the discloser's business. A significantly shorter confidentiality period may no longer be considered necessary and reasonable, thus creating a clear conflict. The modified duration compromises the enforceability and intention of the original agreement, fitting the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the intern al laws of the State of California, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from 'California' to 'Delaware' law creates an in-text contradiction with the jurisdiction clause in section 9. It makes Section 9 ambiguous by conflicting with the specified jurisdiction, creating legal uncertainty and disputes over where legal proceedings should occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the intern al laws of the State of California, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in The United States of America and worldwide. The federal and state courts within the State of California shall have excl usive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from California to Delaware. The contradicted text states that the agreement is governed by California law and that California courts have exclusive jurisdiction. This creates a direct conflict because the agreement cannot be governed by both California and Delaware law simultaneously, leading to uncertainty about which jurisdiction applies, aligning with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, after proving actual damages.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irreparable injury...without the necessity of proving actual damages' to 'injury...after proving actual damages' creates an in-text contradiction. This contradicts the earlier statement in the same section that monetary damages are inadequate. Requiring proof of actual damages before obtaining injunctive relief undermines the assertion that monetary damages are insufficient, creating uncertainty about the Discloser's ability to obtain injunctive relief, thus making it weaker.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that its obliga tions hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. A ccordingly, Recipient agrees an d acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, th e Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, after proving actual damages.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Discloser does not need to prove actual damages to obtain injunctive relief, emphasizing the inadequacy of monetary damages. The changed text introduces the requirement of proving actual damages before obtaining injunctive relief. This directly contradicts the statement in the 'Contradicted Text' that 'monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser'. The change fundamentally alters the conditions under which the Discloser can seek injunctive relief, creating a strong in-text contradiction as it undermines the purpose of injunctive relief by requiring proof of damages that are explicitly stated to be inadequate.
